teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** Under section 2(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, a person cannot, by reference to any contract term or to a notice given to persons generally or to particular persons, exclude or restrict liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence. This is an absolute prohibition — the requirement of reasonableness has no application to s.2(1). For other loss or damage (e.g. property damage or pure economic loss), s.2(2) permits exclusion or restriction, but only insofar as the term satisfies the requirement of reasonableness under s.11.

**The Definition of Negligence (s.1):** UCTA adopts a broad definition. Negligence means the breach of (a) any obligation, arising from the express or implied terms of a contract, to take reasonable care or exercise reasonable skill; (b) any common law duty to take reasonable care (i.e. the tort of negligence); or (c) the common duty of care imposed by the Occupiers' Liability Act 1957. This breadth means UCTA catches exclusion clauses in professional services, maintenance contracts, and occupiers' liability notices.

**Smith v Eric S Bush [1990] 1 AC 831:** A building society instructed surveyors to value a house. The purchaser paid a fee and received a copy of the report containing a disclaimer of liability. The House of Lords held the disclaimer ineffective under s.2(2) because it failed the reasonableness test. Key factors: the surveyor was better placed to bear the risk, there was inequality of bargaining power, and the purchaser relied on the report. The disclaimer was unreasonable and therefore void.

**In short:** Death or personal injury from negligence — liability cannot be excluded. Other loss — can be excluded only if reasonable. *Smith v Eric S Bush* shows courts apply the reasonableness test rigorously against professional defendants.
oscoaReferences: Smith v Eric S Bush [1990] 1 AC 831

question: Under section 2(1) of the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, a person cannot exclude or restrict liability for death or __________ injury resulting from negligence.
feedback: Correct. UCTA 1977 s.2(1) imposes an absolute ban on excluding liability for death or personal injury from negligence. No test of reasonableness applies — it is a complete prohibition.

question: _________ v Eric S Bush [1990] 1 AC 831 established that a surveyor's disclaimer of liability to a residential purchaser was unreasonable and ineffective under UCTA 1977, s.2(2).
feedback: Correct. *Smith v Eric S Bush* [1990] 1 AC 831 — the House of Lords applied the reasonableness test under s.11 and held the disclaimer failed. The purchaser had relied on the valuation and there was no equality of bargaining power.
oscoaReferences: Smith v Eric S Bush [1990] 1 AC 831

question: Match each UCTA 1977 provision to its effect:
answer: Section 2(1)=Absolute prohibition — cannot exclude liability for death or personal injury from negligence; Section 2(2)=Can exclude liability for other loss or damage from negligence, but only if reasonable; Section 1=Defines negligence broadly to include contractual duties, common law duties, and occupiers' liability

options.text: Absolute prohibition — cannot exclude liability for death or personal injury from negligence
Can exclude liability for other loss or damage from negligence, but only if reasonable
Defines negligence broadly to include contractual duties, common law duties, and occupiers' liability

feedback: Well matched. s.2(1) is absolute; s.2(2) is subject to reasonableness; s.1 provides the overarching definition of negligence that activates both subsections.

question: Under UCTA 1977, s.1, negligence includes the breach of the common duty of care imposed by the __________ Liability Act 1957.
feedback: Correct. UCTA s.1(c) brings occupiers' liability within the Act's definition of negligence. This means exclusion notices in premises (e.g. car parks, building sites) are subject to the same s.2 controls.

question: A climbing centre requires all participants to sign a waiver: 'The centre accepts no liability for any injury, including fatal injury, howsoever caused.' Tom signs, then is killed when a negligently maintained rope snaps. His widow sues. The centre relies on the waiver. Under UCTA 1977, s.2(1):

options.text: The waiver is effective because Tom signed it voluntarily and was warned of the risk
options.feedback: Incorrect. UCTA s.2(1) provides an absolute prohibition on excluding liability for death or personal injury from negligence. Voluntary assumption of risk is a separate common law defence (volenti), but the contractual exclusion itself is void.

options.text: The waiver is ineffective — UCTA s.2(1) absolutely prohibits the exclusion of liability for death resulting from negligence, regardless of whether the injured party agreed
options.feedback: Correct. The prohibition in s.2(1) is absolute. The existence of the waiver does not defeat the widow's claim based on negligence, because the purported exclusion of liability is void by statute.

options.text: The waiver is effective if the climbing centre can show the term was reasonable under s.11
options.feedback: Incorrect. The requirement of reasonableness applies to s.2(2) (other loss), not s.2(1). For death or personal injury, the exclusion is prohibited outright — reasonableness is irrelevant.

options.text: The waiver is valid because the Occupiers' Liability Act 1957 permits exclusion of liability for recreational activities
options.feedback: Incorrect. The Occupiers' Liability Act 1957 s.2(1) allows exclusion of the common duty of care by agreement or notice, but such exclusions remain subject to UCTA s.2. The statute does not override UCTA.

feedback: UCTA s.2(1) imposes a total ban on excluding liability for death or personal injury caused by negligence. Unlike s.2(2), there is no reasonableness test — the exclusion is simply void. The widow can pursue the claim in negligence and the centre cannot rely on the waiver.

question: Which TWO of the following statements about UCTA 1977, s.2 are correct?

options.text: Section 2(1) imposes an absolute prohibition on excluding liability for death or personal injury from negligence
options.feedback: Correct. The prohibition in s.2(1) is absolute — reasonableness has no bearing. The term is void regardless of context.

options.text: A business can exclude liability for death caused by negligence if the exclusion satisfies the reasonableness test under s.11
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.2(1) does not contain a reasonableness qualification. Only s.2(2) (other loss or damage) incorporates the reasonableness test.

options.text: Negligence under UCTA is limited to the tort of negligence at common law
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.1 defines negligence broadly to include contractual duties of care, the common law tort, and occupiers' liability under the 1957 Act.

options.text: Under s.2(2), liability for other loss or damage caused by negligence can be excluded only if the term satisfies the requirement of reasonableness
options.feedback: Correct. s.2(2) subjects exclusions of liability for non-personal loss (property damage, pure economic loss) to the reasonableness test in s.11. *Smith v Eric S Bush* [1990] is the leading example.

options.text: Section 2 only applies to contractual exclusion clauses, not to non-contractual notices
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.2(1) expressly applies 'by reference to any contract term or to a notice given to persons generally or to particular persons.' Non-contractual notices are covered.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (d). s.2(1) is absolute for death/PI; s.2(2) allows exclusion for other loss only if reasonable. Negligence is broadly defined in s.1, and s.2 covers notices as well as contract terms.

question: Under UCTA 1977, s.2(2), a person cannot exclude liability for other loss or damage from negligence except insofar as the term satisfies the requirement of __________.
feedback: Correct. s.2(2) permits exclusions for loss other than death or personal injury, but only if the term satisfies the reasonableness test under s.11 and (where applicable) Schedule 2. *Smith v Eric S Bush* [1990] — the surveyor's disclaimer failed this test.
oscoaReferences: Smith v Eric S Bush [1990] 1 AC 831

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** UCTA 1977, s.3 applies where one party deals as a consumer or on the other's written standard terms of business. In these situations, the party relying on the standard terms cannot (a) exclude or restrict liability for breach of contract unless the term satisfies the requirement of reasonableness, or (b) claim to be entitled to render a contractual performance substantially different from that which was reasonably expected, or to render no performance at all, unless the term satisfies the requirement of reasonableness.

**Why s.3 Matters:** Section 3 catches a broader category of unfair terms than s.2. It applies to all contractual breaches — not just those sounding in negligence. A supplier on written standard terms cannot exclude liability for breach unless it is reasonable to do so. This is particularly important in IT contracts, construction, and service industries where standard terms are ubiquitous.

**Pegler v Wang (UK) Ltd [2000] BLR 218:** Wang supplied a computer system on its standard terms, which included a clause excluding liability for all consequential loss and capping liability at the contract price. The system failed. The court held the exclusion clause was unreasonable under s.3(2)(a) because the supplier had insurance against precisely this risk and the limitation was disproportionate. The clause was struck down.

**In short:** If you deal on standard terms or with a consumer, you cannot exclude liability for breach unless reasonable — and you cannot reserve the right to do something completely different from what was promised.

question: Under UCTA 1977, s.3(2)(a), a party dealing on written standard terms cannot exclude or restrict liability for __________ of contract unless the term satisfies the requirement of reasonableness.
feedback: Correct. s.3(2)(a) subjects exclusions of liability for breach to the reasonableness test where one party deals as a consumer or on the other's written standard terms. This is a key protection for the weaker party.

question: __________ v Wang (UK) Ltd [2000] BLR 218 is authority that an exclusion clause in standard terms of business was unreasonable under UCTA s.3, where the supplier had insurance for the risk.
feedback: Correct. *Pegler v Wang (UK) Ltd* [2000] BLR 218 — the court assessed the reasonableness of a limitation clause under s.3, taking into account the availability of insurance, the relative bargaining positions, and the severity of the limitation in the context of the contract.

question: Match each UCTA s.3 provision to its effect:
answer: Section 3(2)(a)=Cannot exclude or restrict liability for breach of contract unless reasonable; Section 3(2)(b)=Cannot claim right to render substantially different performance, or no performance, unless reasonable; Section 3(1) scope=Applies where one party deals as consumer or on the other's written standard terms of business

options.text: Cannot exclude or restrict liability for breach of contract unless reasonable
Cannot claim right to render substantially different performance, or no performance, unless reasonable
Applies where one party deals as consumer or on the other's written standard terms of business

feedback: Perfect. s.3(1) determines when the section bites — consumer contracts and standard terms. s.3(2)(a) controls exclusion of liability for breach. s.3(2)(b) controls terms that permit the supplier to deliver something fundamentally different from what was promised.

question: Under UCTA 1977, s.3(2)(b), a party cannot claim to be entitled to render a performance substantially __________ from that which was reasonably expected, unless the term satisfies the reasonableness test.
feedback: Correct. s.3(2)(b) prevents a supplier from relying on a term that allows a performance fundamentally different from what was contracted for — sometimes called a 'non-performance' or 'different performance' clause — unless the term is reasonable.

question: SoftCo Ltd provides payroll software to a recruitment agency on SoftCo's standard terms, which state: 'SoftCo's total liability for any claim shall not exceed the annual licence fee paid by the Customer (£5,000).' The software malfunctions, causing the agency to miss payroll for 200 contractors and incur £150,000 in penalties. Applying UCTA s.3 and *Pegler v Wang* [2000]:

options.text: The limitation clause is binding because it was in writing and the agency signed the contract
options.feedback: Incorrect. Signing a contract does not insulate an unreasonable term from challenge. UCTA s.3 expressly subjects standard-term exclusions to the reasonableness test regardless of signature.

options.text: The limitation clause automatically fails because all limitation clauses are void under UCTA
options.feedback: Incorrect. UCTA does not render all limitation clauses void. It subjects them to a reasonableness test. Some limitation clauses may be reasonable and therefore enforceable.

options.text: The clause is likely unreasonable under s.3(2)(a) following *Pegler v Wang* — the £5,000 cap is grossly disproportionate to the foreseeable loss, and a software provider should carry appropriate insurance
options.feedback: Correct. Under *Pegler v Wang*, the court will assess reasonableness by considering insurance availability, the proportionality of the cap, and the relative bargaining positions. A £5,000 cap against £150,000 foreseeable loss is likely unreasonable.

options.text: The clause is effective because the agency is a business, not a consumer, and UCTA s.3 does not apply to business-to-business contracts
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.3 applies whenever one party deals on the other's written standard terms of business — including B2B contracts. Consumer status is only one trigger; standard terms are the other.

feedback: Under *Pegler v Wang (UK) Ltd* [2000] BLR 218, the court applies the s.11 reasonableness factors: the availability of insurance, the bargaining strength of the parties, whether the customer knew of the term, and whether the limitation was proportionate. A cap at 3.3% of the loss is very unlikely to be reasonable.

question: Which TWO of the following correctly describe the scope and effect of UCTA 1977, s.3?

options.text: Section 3 only applies where the claimant is a consumer
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.3(1) has two alternative triggers: dealing as a *consumer* or dealing on the other party's *written standard terms of business*. It applies to many B2B contracts.

options.text: Section 3(2)(b) prevents a party from using a term to render a performance substantially different from what was reasonably expected, unless the term is reasonable
options.feedback: Correct. s.3(2)(b) catches clauses that allow a supplier unilaterally to alter the nature of performance — sometimes called variation or non-performance clauses — and subjects them to the reasonableness test.

options.text: A limitation clause in standard terms is automatically void under s.3
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.3 does not void clauses automatically; it subjects them to the reasonableness test. A clause may survive if it is shown to be reasonable in all the circumstances.

options.text: Section 3(2)(a) requires that any exclusion or restriction of liability for breach of contract satisfies the requirement of reasonableness
options.feedback: Correct. Under s.3(2)(a), exclusions of liability for breach in standard-term or consumer contracts must pass the s.11 reasonableness test.

options.text: Section 3 only applies to exclusion clauses, not to limitation clauses
options.feedback: Incorrect. The Act explicitly covers both exclusion and restriction (limitation) of liability. s.13(1) provides that 'exclude or restrict' includes limiting liability to a specified sum.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (d). s.3 applies in both consumer and standard-term contexts, catches both exclusion and limitation clauses, and subjects them to the reasonableness test — it does not void them automatically.

question: EventPro Ltd hires sound equipment from HireCo on HireCo's standard terms. The contract includes: 'HireCo may substitute any item of equipment with an item of equivalent specification.' The key speakers hired are replaced with lower-wattage models that are inadequate for the venue. EventPro refuses to pay, relying on UCTA s.3(2)(b). HireCo says the substitution clause is valid. Is EventPro correct?

options.text: Yes — s.3(2)(b) subjects the substitution clause to the reasonableness test, and replacing the core equipment with inadequate alternatives is likely unreasonable
options.feedback: Correct. The substitution clause permits a different performance. Under s.3(2)(b), it is only enforceable if reasonable. Substituting the key item of the contract with an inferior equivalent is prima facie unreasonable.

options.text: No — substitution clauses are excluded from UCTA because they relate to performance obligations, not exclusion of liability
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.3(2)(b) is specifically aimed at terms that alter the character of performance. A clause permitting substitution of inadequate equipment falls squarely within its scope.

options.text: No — UCTA only applies to consumer contracts and EventPro is a business
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.3 applies where one party deals on the other's written standard terms. EventPro dealt on HireCo's standard terms, so s.3 applies, regardless of EventPro's business status.

options.text: Yes — EventPro can invoke s.2(1) because the inadequate speakers could have caused injury
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.2(1) relates to death or personal injury from negligence, not to contractual performance disputes. The correct provision is s.3(2)(b).

feedback: Section 3(2)(b) is specifically designed to catch terms permitting the supplier to render something other than the promised performance. The substitution clause allows HireCo to deliver a 'substantially different' performance — lower-wattage speakers — and must satisfy the reasonableness test. It is unlikely to do so.

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** UCTA 1977, ss.6 and 7 regulate exclusion clauses in contracts for the sale of goods, hire purchase, and other contracts for the supply of goods. These sections override the general provisions in s.2 and s.3 where goods are involved — they provide a stricter regime.

**Section 6 — Sale of Goods:** s.6(1) provides that liability for breach of the obligations arising under s.12 of the Sale of Goods Act 1979 (the seller's implied undertaking as to title) cannot be excluded or restricted by reference to any contract term. This is an absolute ban — you cannot contract out of the obligation to pass good title. s.6(2) provides that, as against a person dealing as consumer, liability for breach of the obligations arising under ss.13-15 SGA 1979 (correspondence with description, satisfactory quality, and fitness for purpose) cannot be excluded or restricted. Against a non-consumer (s.6(3)), liability for ss.13-15 can be excluded or restricted, but only insofar as the term satisfies the requirement of reasonableness.

**Section 7 — Hire Purchase and Other Supply:** s.7 mirrors s.6 for hire purchase contracts and other contracts under which possession or ownership of goods passes. Title obligations (s.7(1)) cannot be excluded. For consumers, implied terms about quality, fitness, and description cannot be excluded (s.7(2)). For non-consumers, they are subject to the reasonableness test.

**In short:** If you sell goods, you cannot exclude the obligation to pass good title. If you sell to a consumer, you cannot exclude the core quality obligations. If you sell to a business, you can — but only if reasonable.
oscoaReferences: Supply of Goods and Services Act 1982

question: Under UCTA 1977, s.6(1), liability for breach of the implied obligations under s.12 of the Sale of Goods Act 1979 (as to __________) cannot be excluded or restricted at all.
feedback: Correct. s.6(1) imposes an absolute prohibition on excluding the implied undertaking as to title under SGA 1979, s.12. A seller cannot sell goods it does not own and then disclaim liability for the buyer's loss of the goods.

question: Under UCTA 1977, s.6(2), as against a person dealing as __________, liability for breach of the obligations under ss.13-15 of the Sale of Goods Act 1979 cannot be excluded or restricted.
feedback: Correct. s.6(2) provides absolute protection for consumers: a seller cannot exclude liability for goods failing to correspond with description (s.13), failing to be of satisfactory quality (s.14), or failing to be fit for purpose (s.15).

question: Match each UCTA s.6 provision to its effect on exclusion clauses in sale of goods contracts:
answer: Section 6(1)=Liability for breach of s.12 SGA (title) — cannot be excluded at all; Section 6(2)=Liability for breach of ss.13-15 SGA (quality/description/fitness) against consumer — cannot be excluded; Section 6(3)=Liability for breach of ss.13-15 SGA against non-consumer — can be excluded only if reasonable

options.text: Liability for breach of s.12 SGA (title) — cannot be excluded at all
Liability for breach of ss.13-15 SGA (quality/description/fitness) against consumer — cannot be excluded
Liability for breach of ss.13-15 SGA against non-consumer — can be excluded only if reasonable

feedback: Well matched. The hierarchy is: title = absolute ban; consumer quality = absolute ban; non-consumer quality = reasonableness. This structure reflects the policy that certain obligations are too fundamental to contract out of.

question: UCTA 1977, s.7 applies the same framework as s.6 to contracts of __________ purchase and other contracts for the supply of goods.
feedback: Correct. s.7 mirrors s.6 for hire purchase agreements and other contracts under which possession or ownership of goods passes. Title obligations cannot be excluded, and quality obligations follow the consumer/non-consumer divide.
oscoaReferences: Supply of Goods and Services Act 1982

question: Which TWO of the following correctly describe the effect of UCTA 1977, ss.6-7?

options.text: A seller of goods to a consumer cannot exclude liability for breach of the implied terms as to satisfactory quality and fitness for purpose
options.feedback: Correct. s.6(2) provides an absolute prohibition on excluding ss.13-15 SGA obligations against consumers. The protection cannot be contracted out of.

options.text: A business seller can always exclude liability for defective goods if it includes an 'as is, as seen' clause in the contract
options.feedback: Incorrect. Against a consumer, 'as is' clauses are ineffective (s.6(2)). Against a non-consumer, such clauses must pass the reasonableness test (s.6(3)). An 'as is' clause is not automatically valid.

options.text: Liability for breach of the seller's implied obligation to pass good title under s.12 SGA 1979 cannot be excluded under any circumstances
options.feedback: Correct. s.6(1) imposes an absolute ban on excluding title obligations. This applies to all contracts for the sale of goods, regardless of the parties' status.

options.text: Section 7 only applies to hire purchase contracts with consumers
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.7 applies to hire purchase and other supply contracts between all parties, including B2B contracts. The consumer/non-consumer distinction affects whether the ban is absolute or subject to reasonableness.

options.text: A non-consumer buyer can challenge an exclusion clause under ss.13-15 SGA only if the seller acted fraudulently
options.feedback: Incorrect. Non-consumer buyers can challenge exclusion clauses under s.6(3) by showing the clause is unreasonable. There is no requirement to prove fraud.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (c). Title obligations are sacrosanct (s.6(1)). Consumer quality obligations are similarly untouchable (s.6(2)). Non-consumer quality exclusions are permissible — but only if reasonable under s.11.

question: BakeRight Ltd, a commercial bakery, buys a £50,000 industrial oven from OvenCo on OvenCo's standard terms: 'OvenCo excludes all liability for defects in quality, fitness for purpose, or performance. The buyer's sole remedy is repair or replacement at OvenCo's discretion.' The oven is unfit for commercial baking and OvenCo refuses to replace it. Applying UCTA s.6(3):

options.text: BakeRight cannot challenge the clause because it is a business, not a consumer, and UCTA only protects consumers
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.6(3) expressly applies to non-consumer contracts. Business buyers can challenge exclusions of ss.13-15 SGA obligations, but the test is reasonableness rather than an absolute ban.

options.text: The clause is absolutely void under s.6(1) because the oven was not fit for purpose
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.6(1) relates to title (s.12 SGA), not quality. Fitness for purpose is governed by s.6(3) for non-consumers — the exclusion is subject to reasonableness, not an absolute ban.

options.text: BakeRight can challenge the clause under s.6(3) — the exclusion of all liability for quality and fitness must satisfy the reasonableness test, and an exclusion that leaves the buyer with no effective remedy is likely unreasonable
options.feedback: Correct. s.6(3) requires the exclusion to be reasonable. A complete exclusion of liability for the core qualities of the goods, in a contract for a high-value commercial item, is likely to fail the reasonableness test.

options.text: The exclusion is valid because the remedy of repair/replacement is adequate in all circumstances
options.feedback: Incorrect. The clause gives OvenCo discretion to refuse even repair/replacement. The clause is not a genuine alternative remedy but a complete exclusion of substantive liability. Under s.6(3), its reasonableness is highly doubtful.

feedback: Under s.6(3), the exclusion of liability for quality and fitness must be reasonable. The court would consider the factors in s.11 and Schedule 2: the relative bargaining positions, whether BakeRight could have bought the oven elsewhere without the exclusion, and whether the clause was brought to BakeRight's attention. A blanket exclusion with no effective remedy is unlikely to be reasonable.

question: Terry buys a used car from a dealer for £3,000. The sales invoice states: 'Sold as seen. No warranty given as to condition, quality, or fitness for any purpose.' The car's engine seizes after two days due to a pre-existing defect. The dealer argues the exclusion clause bars Terry's claim. Under UCTA s.6(2):

options.text: The exclusion clause is void — s.6(2) absolutely prohibits exclusion of the ss.13-15 SGA obligations against a consumer, including 'sold as seen' clauses
options.feedback: Correct. Terry is a consumer. s.6(2) renders ineffective any attempt to exclude liability for breach of the implied terms as to satisfactory quality and fitness for purpose. The 'sold as seen' clause is void.

options.text: The clause is effective, provided the dealer can show it was reasonable in the circumstances
options.feedback: Incorrect. The reasonableness test applies to non-consumer contracts (s.6(3)), not consumer contracts. Against a consumer, the exclusion is absolutely prohibited.

options.text: Terry must rely on the Consumer Rights Act 2015 because UCTA does not apply to used goods
options.feedback: Incorrect. UCTA s.6(2) applies to all sales of goods to consumers, new or used. The CRA 2015 also applies and provides similar protections, but UCTA remains in force for the relevant obligations.

options.text: The clause is saved by s.7 because cars are supplied on hire purchase terms
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.7 applies to hire purchase and analogous supply contracts — not to outright sales. An outright car sale is governed by s.6.

feedback: UCTA s.6(2) provides absolute protection for consumers against exclusion of the SGA quality obligations. 'Sold as seen' clauses are specifically caught and rendered void. The dealer cannot contract out of the obligation to supply goods of satisfactory quality.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] A gym requires members to sign: 'The gym accepts no responsibility for any injury or death arising from use of facilities.' A member is seriously injured when a negligently maintained treadmill malfunctions. The gym relies on the waiver. Under UCTA s.2(1):

options.text: The waiver is effective because the member assumed the risk by signing
options.feedback: Incorrect. Voluntary assumption of risk (volenti) is a distinct common law defence, but UCTA s.2(1) provides a statutory bar that overrides contractual exclusions for death or personal injury from negligence.

options.text: The waiver is void — UCTA s.2(1) imposes an absolute prohibition on excluding liability for personal injury caused by negligence, and the reasonableness of the clause is irrelevant
options.feedback: Correct. s.2(1) is absolute. The waiver is ineffective regardless of whether the member signed it, read it, or understood it. The prohibition applies to both contract terms and notices.

options.text: The gym can rely on the waiver if it can prove the treadmill was serviced regularly
options.feedback: Incorrect. Evidence of maintenance goes to whether there was negligence, not whether an exclusion of liability for that negligence is valid. If there was negligence, s.2(1) prevents exclusion regardless of precautions taken.

options.text: The waiver is effective for injury but not for death under s.2(1)
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.2(1) applies to both death and personal injury. The prohibition is the same for both.

feedback: This consolidates Block A: the absolute prohibition in s.2(1). Death or personal injury from negligence cannot be excluded — not by contract term and not by notice. The waiver is void.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] In __________ v Eric S Bush [1990] 1 AC 831, the House of Lords applied UCTA s.2(2) and held that a surveyor's disclaimer was unreasonable and ineffective.
feedback: Correct. *Smith v Eric S Bush* [1990] 1 AC 831 — this is the leading authority on the application of the reasonableness test under s.2(2) to professional negligence disclaimers. It consolidates the principle that s.2(2) does not save unreasonable exclusions.
oscoaReferences: Smith v Eric S Bush [1990] 1 AC 831

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block B] TechBuild Ltd supplies bespoke CRM software to an estate agency on TechBuild's standard terms, which state: 'TechBuild may suspend or discontinue support for any module at any time without notice or compensation.' TechBuild discontinues a critical module six weeks after deployment. The agency sues. Applying UCTA s.3(2)(b) and *Pegler v Wang* [2000]:

options.text: The clause is valid — the agency should have objected to the standard terms before signing
options.feedback: Incorrect. The fact that terms are in writing and signed does not preclude a challenge under UCTA. The statute specifically exists to police unfair terms in such circumstances.

options.text: The agency has no claim because the right to discontinue is a performance obligation, not an exclusion of liability
options.feedback: Incorrect. s.3(2)(b) is precisely aimed at terms that permit a party to render a different performance — including no performance. The right to discontinue support is within the provision's scope.

options.text: The clause is subject to the reasonableness test under s.3(2)(b) — a right to withdraw critical functionality without notice or compensation is likely unreasonable
options.feedback: Correct. The clause arrogates to TechBuild the right to render a performance substantially different from that reasonably expected — i.e. a CRM without its critical module. It must pass the reasonableness test, and a unilateral right to withdraw key functionality without compensation is improbable to satisfy it.

options.text: The clause is void under s.6 because software is a 'good' within the Sale of Goods Act
options.feedback: Incorrect. Software is generally not treated as 'goods' under the SGA 1979 (*St Albans City and District Council v International Computers Ltd* [1996]). The correct UCTA provision for a standard-term services/software contract is s.3.

feedback: This consolidates Block B. s.3(2)(b) catches terms that allow the supplier unilaterally to alter the substance of the performance. Following *Pegler v Wang* [2000], the court will assess whether the discretion to discontinue is reasonable — weighing the degree of the buyer's reliance, the proportionality of the term, and the supplier's justification.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Blocks A & B] Which TWO of the following correctly describe the relationship between UCTA ss.2, 3, and 6?

options.text: Section 2 is the primary provision; ss.3 and 6 only apply if s.2 does not
options.feedback: Incorrect. ss.2, 3, and 6 operate independently and can overlap. Where goods are involved, s.6 takes priority over s.2 for matters within its scope, but the provisions are not hierarchical in the sense of one being 'primary.'

options.text: Section 2 governs negligence liability generally; s.3 governs standard-term and consumer contracts; s.6 specifically governs sale of goods contracts — and s.6 may apply instead of s.2 where goods are concerned
options.feedback: Correct. The provisions address different domains: s.2 (negligence), s.3 (standard terms/consumer), s.6 (sale of goods). By s.6(4), s.2 does not apply to the obligations in respect of which s.6 operates.

options.text: All three sections contain identical tests — an absolute prohibition on exclusion of liability
options.feedback: Incorrect. Only s.2(1) and s.6(1)-(2) contain absolute prohibitions. s.2(2), s.3, s.6(3), and s.7 all incorporate a reasonableness qualification.

options.text: Section 3 can apply to standard-term contracts for services; s.6 applies specifically to contracts for the sale of goods
options.feedback: Correct. s.3 is the residual provision for services and other contracts not covered by ss.6 or 7. For the sale of goods, s.6 provides a lex specialis regime.

options.text: Section 3 only applies where the claimant has suffered death or personal injury
options.feedback: Incorrect. Death and personal injury are the domain of s.2(1). s.3 applies to any breach of contract where one party deals on standard terms or as consumer, regardless of the type of loss.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (d). The UCTA provisions form a coherent scheme: s.2 covers negligence (including occupiers' liability); s.3 covers contractual breaches on standard terms; ss.6-7 provide a specialised regime for the sale and supply of goods. Where ss.6-7 apply, they displace s.2.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block C] Under UCTA ss.6-7, the key distinction is whether the buyer or hirer is a __________: if so, the core quality obligations cannot be excluded; if not, the exclusion is subject to the reasonableness test.
feedback: Correct. The consumer/non-consumer distinction is the organising principle of ss.6-7. Consumers receive absolute protection; non-consumers (businesses) must show the exclusion is unreasonable. This consolidates the Block C material.
